name: Platform Neutrality Mandate
consequences: Immediate: Policy brief released → Systemic: 30% increase in procurement clauses referencing platform‑neutral fallback → Strategic: Lowers Apple/Google reliance by 40% while exposing political resistance.
options: Introduce a voluntary guideline for new AltID contracts to consider platform-neutral designs.
Require all new public‑sector authentication contracts to include a certified fallback path using open standards, with a 2‑year sunset for legacy Apple/Google reliance.
Enact a statutory requirement that no public identity system may depend exclusively on Apple or Google integrity APIs, with a 12‑month compliance window for existing services.
review: Controls supplier concentration versus political feasibility. Weakness: The options lack a clear timeline for phasing out existing Apple/Google contracts.

name: Fallback Authentication Demonstrator
consequences: Immediate: Prototype demo deployed → Systemic: 50% reduction in reliance on proprietary push‑notification services → Strategic: Shows viable fallback path while limiting cross‑platform compatibility.
options: Build a proof‑of‑concept using existing open‑source FIDO2 libraries on a commercial Android device.
Develop a GrapheneOS‑based mobile client that runs on a privacy‑focused OS and supports open standards.
Create a browser‑based, hardware‑token fallback that works on any device without OS‑specific APIs, leveraging WebAuthn and decentralized identity wallets.
review: Controls technical feasibility versus user experience trade‑off. Weakness: The demonstrators may not scale to high‑volume public authentication loads.

name: Procurement Language Injection
consequences: Immediate: Draft language circulated to procurement office → Systemic: 20% of upcoming RFPs adopt fallback requirements → Strategic: Institutionalizes resilience but risks budgetary pushback from agencies.
options: Suggest adding a clause about “considering alternative trust anchors” in RFPs.
Draft procurement language mandating a certified fallback authentication path for all new identity services.
Require all future Danish digital identity contracts to embed platform‑neutral fallback specifications and audit mechanisms, with penalties for non‑compliance.
review: Controls procurement influence versus budgetary constraints. Weakness: The language may be too prescriptive to gain agency acceptance.

name: EU Standards Alignment
consequences: Immediate: Position paper submitted → Systemic: 15% increase in EU policy attention to fallback mechanisms → Strategic: Amplifies leverage at EU level while risking slower national implementation.
options: Participate in EU working groups to comment on draft wallet specifications.
Co‑author a position paper aligning Danish fallback requirements with EU interoperability goals.
Lead coalition to propose amendment to EU Digital Identity Regulation that explicitly requires certified fallback mechanisms for member‑state wallets.
review: Controls EU alignment versus national sovereignty concerns. Weakness: The amendment effort may be diluted by broader EU negotiations.

name: Coalition Mobilization
consequences: Immediate: Coalition statement published → Systemic: 10% rise in parliamentary inquiries on supplier concentration → Strategic: Builds political pressure but may provoke defensive reactions from incumbents.
options: Organize a round‑table with civil‑society groups to raise awareness.
Secure endorsements from at least three major privacy NGOs and two academic institutions.
Launch a public campaign framing platform neutrality as a national security imperative, mobilizing media and parliamentary allies.
review: Controls coalition breadth versus message clarity. Weakness: The radical campaign risks alienating moderate stakeholders.

name: Legislative Platform Neutrality Directive
consequences: Immediate: Policy note released → Systemic: 15% increase in procurement clauses referencing platform‑neutral fallback → Strategic: Lowers Apple/Google reliance by 30% within 18 months, balancing speed of adoption against political resistance.
options: Publish a non‑binding policy note encouraging platform‑neutral designs in AltID contracts.
Issue an executive order that requires new public authentication contracts to include a certified fallback path.
Pass a parliamentary act that obliges all public identity systems to implement a certified fallback within 24 months.
review: Controls political feasibility versus regulatory speed. Weakness: The options lack a clear enforcement mechanism for compliance.

name: Dedicated Fallback Funding Pool
consequences: Immediate: Funding line established → Systemic: 20% boost in prototype development capacity → Strategic: Accelerates fallback demonstrators by 40% while requiring re‑allocation of existing budget, trading off immediate fiscal flexibility against long‑term resilience.
options: Reserve 5% of the project’s annual budget for fallback research and prototype testing.
Create a matching fund where private partners contribute equally to public grants for fallback solutions.
Establish a revolving grant program that finances open‑source fallback authentication modules.
review: Controls fiscal allocation versus technical agility. Weakness: The funding mechanisms may be constrained by existing budget caps and parliamentary approval processes.

name: Procurement Language Injection
consequences: Immediate: Draft language circulated to procurement office → Systemic: 25% of upcoming RFPs adopt fallback requirement → Strategic: Institutionalizes resilience, though it may trigger budgetary pushback from agencies, weighing institutional buy‑in against cost.
options: Add a clause to RFPs that asks bidders to describe alternative trust anchors they will consider.
Require bidders to demonstrate a certified fallback authentication path as part of the qualification criteria.
Make platform‑neutral fallback a mandatory pre‑qualification condition, with financial penalties for non‑compliance.
review: Controls procurement influence versus agency autonomy. Weakness: The mandatory fallback clause could be challenged as overly prescriptive and stall procurement cycles.

name: EU Standards Alignment
consequences: Immediate: Position paper submitted → Systemic: 10% rise in EU policy attention to fallback mechanisms → Strategic: Amplifies external pressure while risking delayed national rollout, trading off EU influence against domestic sovereignty concerns.
options: Submit technical comments to the EU wallet working group on the need for fallback mechanisms.
Co‑author a joint Nordic position paper aligning Danish fallback requirements with EU interoperability goals.
Propose an amendment to the EU Digital Identity Regulation that explicitly mandates certified fallback for member‑state wallets.
review: Controls EU alignment versus national policy sovereignty. Weakness: The amendment effort may be diluted by broader EU negotiations and lose impact.

name: Coalition Mobilization & Messaging
consequences: Immediate: Coalition statement published → Systemic: 12% increase in parliamentary inquiries on supplier concentration → Strategic: Generates political pressure but may alienate moderate stakeholders, balancing advocacy intensity against coalition cohesion.
options: Convene a round‑table with civil‑society groups to raise awareness of supplier concentration risks.
Obtain public endorsements from three privacy NGOs and two leading academic research centers.
Launch a national media campaign that frames platform neutrality as a matter of national security.
review: Controls coalition breadth versus message clarity. Weakness: The radical campaign risk may alienate moderate stakeholders and reduce bipartisan support.

Issue an executive order requiring new public authentication contracts to include a certified fallback path.
Pass a parliamentary act that mandates a certified fallback architecture using open‑source trust‑anchor marketplaces, creating a competitive ecosystem for identity service providers within 24 months.
